content:
Bayesian updating traces to **Thomas Bayes** (18th century) and **Pierre Laplace**, who treated probability as rational belief updated by evidence—P(θ|x) ∝ P(x|θ)P(θ) is their legacy in modern notation.

**Frequentist** inference later emphasized sample-based estimators without explicit priors, though many procedures (regularization, empirical Bayes) reintroduce prior-like terms.

**20th-century** computation (MCMC, variational inference) made approximate posteriors practical for models where closed forms fail.

In modern ML, priors appear openly in MAP/Ridge interpretations, Bayesian neural nets, and as the conceptual target behind ELBO-based variational methods—even when engineers think in terms of loss penalties rather than beliefs.

timeline.label: Bayes & Laplace
timeline.description: Inverse probability: update beliefs with new observations using what we now call priors and posteriors.

timeline.label: Penalties as priors
timeline.description: Ridge/Lasso and MAP show classical regularization is implicit prior specification on parameters.

timeline.label: Approximate inference
timeline.description: MCMC and variational methods scale posterior thinking to deep models without closed-form updates.
